On one hand, I like the idea to have these names to potentially expose [Scunthorpe problems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scunthorpe_problem).

On the other hand, the lists those names belongs to don't reference any source, so there isn't any factual data telling us how common they are.

I have looked into this further and have made some important findings.

As a test scenario, I extended the Tests.test_likelihood_fitting_gaussian in test_general.py. Here we sample N points from a normal distribution with known mean mu and standard deviation sigma. We then do a likelihood fit with a gaussian distribution to this sample and find back the known parameters mu and sigma:

```python
mu, sig = parameters('mu, sig')
sig.value = 3.0
mu.value = 50.0
N = 10000
x = Variable()
pdf = Gaussian(x, mu, sig)

np.random.seed(10)
xdata = np.random.normal(51., 3.5, N)

# Expected values
mean = np.mean(xdata)
stdev = np.std(xdata)
mean_stdev = stdev / np.sqrt(N)

print(mean, mean_stdev)
fit = Fit(pdf, xdata, objective=LogLikelihood)
fit_result = fit.execute()

self.assertAlmostEqual(fit_result.value(mu) / mean, 1.0)
self.assertAlmostEqual(fit_result.stdev(mu) / mean_stdev, 1.0)
self.assertAlmostEqual(fit_result.value(sig) / stdev, 1.0)
```

But we also know that the uncertainty in mu is given by sigma/sqrt(N), so if we find this in our `FitResults` we are happy.

If I use `ans.hess_inv` as the covariance matrix this seems to be in good agreement, but not exactly, for low N (5-100). However, as I increase N the uncertainty in mu seems to approach a certain asymptote and doesn't change any further.

After reading [this ](https://www.statlect.com/fundamentals-of-statistics/maximum-likelihood-covariance-matrix-estimation) I decided to implement the outer product of gradients (OPG) as specified there, and this given the exact result for all N. Comparing this outer product with `ans.hess_inv` we find the same; it is almost the same at small N but not at large N. It would therefore seem that the `L-BFGS-B` algorithm is unable to give a good estimate or, more likely, that it has something to do with the order of summation, differentiation, and matrix inversion that does not commute since the `L-BFGS-B` algorithm can only work with the scalar value returned by the LogLikelihoog function but to compute the OPG we need the gradient of the likelihood function at every individual point before summing and inverting.

I will therefore proceed to implement the OPG method to estimate the covariance matrix, and I will also add a `NotImplementedError` when trying to add weights to data in likelihood fitting since, well, that has not been implemented (https://arxiv.org/pdf/math/0505599.pdf).

Hi, thank you for pointing out this issue. I noticed this behavior already, but thought of it as a inaccuracy of the algorithm. After your comment I added some tests that allow to compare my results to those of the [`hurstexp` function in the R-package pracma](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/pracma/versions/1.9.9/topics/hurstexp). (see eaeb3c5be9c0ecbd72b6922015a00234e1f6016a)

It seems that values higher than 0.5 are indeed quite normal for white noise. However, my implementation is surely on the higher end of the spectrum. I reviewed the code, but could not find any sources of a real error. I tweaked the default choice for the `nvals` parameter a little bit (see 5fe9357414b879fbc064bf7763f5b3b64c7ebe88), but I think the only thing that could make a real difference is the "correction factor" that is also applied by Weron in his 2002 paper.

I will try to implement this factor in the next version and see if it improves the validity of the return values.

The main problem is that pkg-config support (as is the case with dlib right now) is not completely ubiquitous in the ecosystem. libjpeg, openblas etc. do not have pkg-config support. I'm currently on an updating spree, where I push pkg-config into as main libraries as possible, such that everyone can simplify their build systems. Once these libraries have full pkg-config support, yes, they then should be added to the `REQUIRES_LIBS` variable as you suggested. A stopgap solution is to add the linker arguments for these libraries to the `Libs:` flag, but this is more brittle, as it makes dlib responsible for the linker flags of other packages.

Concerning the `dlib-1.pc`: this is good practice and gives a rough versioning of the API. Imagine you update dlib in an incompatible way. What are users supposed to do that have developed to the older API? A lot of programs won't switch over night, and by having the new API use dlib-2.pc, the old and new library can be installed in parallel. This is more flexible and forward looking. All major libraries do it (GTK+, Pango, etc...) and it has 0 cost. See for instance:
https://developer.gnome.org/programming-guidelines/stable/parallel-installability.html.en

如果不停的用 bypy -e upload 上传整个目录的话， 有md5差异的文件是会不断减少的。 好像是每次第一个有md5差异的总是不再重新上传文件，直接执行combine， 然后就重新生成正确的md5了。    我猜baidu的后台对于分片的临时文件， 可能只针对一个文件， 每次调用 combine（也就是crreatesuperfile接口）后， 这个子目录的其它文件的上次上传的分片文件应该就一并清除了， 所以其它文件内容只能重新上传。

First need to be sure that header files and static libraries for python dev. installed  properly:

sudo apt-get install python3-dev  # for python3.x installs
Then try to install RPIO branch v2, it will work with RPI3.
cd ~
git clone https://github.com/metachris/RPIO.git --branch v2 --single-branch
cd RPIO
sudo python setup.py install

This solution helped me install RPIO for several RPI3s.

With regard to MEDIT's `mesh` format, as defined in §7.2.1 ‘The `mesh` format’ of

* Pascal Frey. MEDIT : An interactive mesh visualization software. RT-0253, INRIA. 2001, pp. 41. [inria-00069921](https://hal.inria.fr/inria-00069921/fr/)

attributes one _ref_ to the syntax for each of the `Vertices`, `Edges`, `Triangles`, `Quadrilaterals`, `Tetrahedra`, and `Hexaedra`.  It doesn't say what this _ref_ is, but I assume it's an integer tag, which is how it's interpreted in _FreeFem++_, for example.

Gmsh writes this with `-format mesh`.  By default (and originally only), it used the elementary (geometric) entity for _ref_, but about a decade ago it got the option `Mesh.SaveElementTagType=2` to use the physical tag; see, e.g., [[Gmsh] Format Mesh and Boundary Conditions](http://onelab.info/pipermail/gmsh/2009/005057.html) (2009-12-03):

> > Maybe there exists a option that allows me to take those physical tags into account ?

> Yes, this was added recently (v. 2.4.2, I think): `Mesh.SaveElementTagType=2`

For example, [t1.mesh.gz](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/files/2334368/t1.mesh.gz) was produced by
```shell
gmsh -2 -format mesh -string 'Mesh.SaveElementTagType=2;' t1.geo
```

In short: looks like exactly one `int` tag for each node and each element.  

That would map straightforwardly to `node_data` and `cell_data`, although a name for the key is required.  For the `cell_data`, the tag is exactly the same as what used to go in `gmsh:physical` (assuming `Mesh.SaveElementTagType=2`) but that's a funny name for a key that might have nothing to do with Gmsh.
I thought I had fixed that. What version are you on? (`print(meshio.__version__)` will tell.)

OK, guess I have why the C ext building was broken:

Because vagrant did not support openindiana for a while and was just recently fixed, my openindiana box was a bit outdated. Some stuff (e.g. current Cython, maybe also scandir?) now wanted gcc-6 which somehow was not there (yet). So my first tries to build that stuff failed when it tried to compile C stuff - but I guess pip cached a wheel for scandir 1.9.0 made at that time (and thus: without _scandir C extension).

Later, i fixed by updating the system and also installing gcc-6.

But, as pip had a cached wheel for scandir 1.9.0, it continued using this and thus missed the C extension.

I killed `~/.cache/pip` later and now a freshly pip-installed scandir 1.9.0 does have the C extension and thus does not trigger the exception and more.

So seems like this rather was a caching issue than a build problem rooted in scandir setup code.

But at least we found a bug, so please merge #111.

```bash
cut --delimiter " " --fields 1-8 KeyFrameNavStateTrajectory.txt > clean.tum
```

This file is then valid according to `evo_traj tum clean.tum --full_check `.

Did you reset the cluster beforehand? 

`auto_offset_reset` only kicks in if there are no offsets stored... as soon as you run the consumer once, it will by default auto-commit an offset. This offset will be used on the next run unless the offset is removed either by resetting the cluster or the offset expiring from the internal offsets topic.

> thanks for setting up another python library for interactive brokers.
>
> Right now it's possible to request historical data on bars and tick data.
> The main problem is that Interactive Brokers is limiting the look-back for
> historical data. Here's a list of limitations:
> http://www.tradingsoftwarelab.com/jtwsdump/table.html
> So the lowest level on bars to request is seconds and here you can
> look-back a maximum of 2000 seconds (~30min).
> But it's possible to request a few month of tick data. You can request a
> maximum of 1000 ticks for each tick-request.
> I'm not familiar with python yet, so I just cannot write it on my own yet
> and make a pull request, but here's the pseudo code:
>
>    - make a tick request for a given start date
>    - take the most recent timestamp received from this request and set it
>    as the new start date for the second request. Loop until now. You will
>    struggle with some pacing violations and you'll have to wait for them to
>    release again. Best is to save all incoming tick data on a csv file for
>    faster lookup.
>    - take the tick data file and build bars out of them in the timeframe
>    you want: 1-sec, 1-min, 22-min, 1hour. IBs data are not the best, so
>    perhaps there is the need to make some winsorizing on them.

The following code works using PyQt/PySide and the last pre-release of CEF Python 51 Beta  on Linux:

```python

import sys
from cefpython3 import cefpython

from PySide.QtGui import *
from PySide.QtCore import *
#from PyQt4.QtGui import *
#from PyQt4.QtCore import *


class MainWindow(QMainWindow):
    mainFrame = None

    def __init__(self):
        super(MainWindow, self).__init__(None)
       
        self.mainFrame = MainFrame(self)
        self.setCentralWidget(self.mainFrame)
        self.resize(1000, 1000)
        self.setFocusPolicy(Qt.StrongFocus)

    def focusInEvent(self, event):
        pass
        
    def closeEvent(self, event):
        self.mainFrame.browser.CloseBrowser()

class MainFrame(QX11EmbedContainer):
    browser = None

    def __init__(self, parent=None):
        super(MainFrame, self).__init__(parent)
        self.x = 0
        self.y = 0
        self.width = 600
        self.height = 600 
        windowInfo = cefpython.WindowInfo()
        windowInfo.SetAsChild(int(self.winId()),[self.x, self.y, self.width,self.height])
        
        self.browser = cefpython.CreateBrowserSync(windowInfo,
                browserSettings={},
                navigateUrl= "http://www.google.com") 
        self.show()

    def moveEvent(self, event):
        pos = event.pos()
        self.x = pos.x()
        self.y = pos.y() 
        self.browser.SetBounds(self.x, self.y, self.width, self.height)
          

    def resizeEvent(self, event):
        size = event.size()
        self.width = size.width()
        self.height = size.height() 
        self.browser.SetBounds(self.x, self.y, self.width, self.height)
       
        

class CefApplication(QApplication):	
    timer = None
    
    def __init__(self, args):
        super(CefApplication, self).__init__(args)
        self.createTimer()
 	
    def createTimer(self):
		self.timer = QTimer()
		self.timer.timeout.connect(self.onTimer)  		
		self.timer.start(10)
    
    def onTimer(self):
        # The proper way of doing message loop should be:
        # 1. In createTimer() call self.timer.start(0)
        # 2. In onTimer() call MessageLoopWork() only when
        #    QtGui.QApplication.instance()->hasPendingEvents() returns False.
        # But... there is a bug in Qt, hasPendingEvents() returns always true.
		cefpython.MessageLoopWork()

    def stopTimer(self):
        # Stop the timer after Qt message loop ended, calls to MessageLoopWork()
        # should not happen anymore.
        self.timer.stop()

if __name__ == '__main__':


    settings = {}
  
    # This directories must be set on Linux
    settings["locales_dir_path"]  = cefpython.GetModuleDirectory()+"/locales"
    settings["resources_dir_path"] = cefpython.GetModuleDirectory()

  
    settings["context_menu"] = {
      "enabled": True,
      "navigation": True, # Back, Forward, Reload
      "print": False,
      "view_source": False,
      "external_browser": False, # Open in external browser
      "devtools": False, # Developer Tools
    }
    settings["browser_subprocess_path"] = "%s/%s" % (cefpython.GetModuleDirectory(), "subprocess")

    
    cefpython.Initialize(settings)
    app = CefApplication(sys.argv)
    mainWindow = MainWindow()
    mainWindow.show()
    app.exec_()
    app.stopTimer()

    # Need to destroy QApplication(), otherwise Shutdown() fails.
    # Unset main window also just to be safe.
    del mainWindow
    del app

    cefpython.Shutdown()
```

That sounds interesting @Suor, but not as easy as it sounds. The current implementation just exposes the interface Django expects at the location it expects it to. There is no monkey patching or other changes to the way Django loads commands.

The change you suggest would involve changing the Django commands discovery and loading mechanism, which is a different problem from what django-click currently solves.

The current implementation is available here:
https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/management/__init__.py#L22-L31
https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/management/__init__.py#L45-L76
As you can see it is almost completely file-system based (hard to do magic stuff with modules loading) and does offer little room to hook some custom logic into it.

Sure, the only thing I can think of for mathematical evaluation is that you should decide ahead of time if you just want to deal with numbers and operators, or a combination of those and words. Such as seven + 8.

For the other questions,

1) I am currently unsure about how to logically determine which algorithm to apply. This feature requires an element of trail and error testing that is not currently present in the program. The main issue stems from the difficulty of measuring the "success" of a given response. For instance the response of "You are welcome" could be considered a _successful_ response to "Thank you", however there is no basis for measuring the value of success without prior experience or a way to verify the _correctness_.

2) I think the concept of the find_a_name method could be refactored into something more general that would allow the program to search a knowledge base for a result and format the findings as a proper sentence. Wikipedia database dumps might be a good source of information to use as a knowledge base.

3) This ticket predates the addition of adapters in ChatterBot, the concept for a custom_method feature was a possible precursor to this. I think in the future, the direction will be moving towards the ability to set multiple logic adapters where some could handle things such as conversations and others could accomplish more complex tasks when the bot is appropriately prompted. Completion of this stage of design would rely a bit on question (1) because there needs to be a way to measure the success of a one response choice against another.

@lukaskubis - When unpickling, the *pickle* library makes some direct calls to `__getattr__` as part of its memoization. Those calls are made before the class instance `__dict__` is instantiated, so when it is unpickling it would call `__getattr__` which internally tried to access `self.currently` - because the class instance did not have a `currently` attribute yet, that attribute access would in turn call `__getattr__(self, 'currently')` which created an infinite recursion, preventing the unpickling from succeeding.

```
import face_recognition
import cv2

# This is a demo of running face recognition on live video from your webcam. It's a little more complicated than the
# other example, but it includes some basic performance tweaks to make things run a lot faster:
#   1. Process each video frame at 1/4 resolution (though still display it at full resolution)
#   2. Only detect faces in every other frame of video.

# PLEASE NOTE: This example requires OpenCV (the `cv2` library) to be installed only to read from your webcam.
# OpenCV is *not* required to use the face_recognition library. It's only required if you want to run this
# specific demo. If you have trouble installing it, try any of the other demos that don't require it instead.

# Get a reference to webcam #0 (the default one)
video_capture = cv2.VideoCapture(0)

# Load a sample picture and learn how to recognize it.
obama_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("WuShiFu.jpg")
obama_face_encoding = face_recognition.face_encodings(obama_image)[0]

# Initialize some variables
face_locations = []
face_encodings = []
face_names = []
process_this_frame = True

while True:
    # Grab a single frame of video
    ret, frame = video_capture.read()

    # Resize frame of video to 1/4 size for faster face recognition processing
    small_frame = cv2.resize(frame, (0, 0), fx=0.25, fy=0.25)

    # Convert the image from BGR color (which OpenCV uses) to RGB color (which face_recognition uses)
    rgb_small_frame = small_frame[:, :, ::-1]

    # Only process every other frame of video to save time
    if process_this_frame:
        # Find all the faces and face encodings in the current frame of video
        face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(rgb_small_frame)
        face_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(rgb_small_frame, face_locations)

        face_names = []
        for face_encoding in face_encodings:
            # See if the face is a match for the known face(s)
            match = face_recognition.compare_faces([obama_face_encoding], face_encoding)
            name = "Unknown"

            if match[0]:
                name = "Barack"

            face_names.append(name)

            distance = face_recognition.face_distance([obama_face_encoding], face_encoding)
            # print("distance of frame from camera: {:.10}.".format(min(distance)))
            print("distance of frame from camera: {}.".format(distance))

            file_name = "D:\\test_distance.jpg"
            cv2.imwrite(file_name, small_frame)
            image = face_recognition.load_image_file(file_name)
            location = face_recognition.face_locations(image)
            encoding = face_recognition.face_encodings(image, location)
            if len(encoding) > 0:
                distance_image = face_recognition.face_distance([obama_face_encoding], encoding[0])
                # print("distance of image from local:  {:.10}.".format(min(distance_image)))
                print("distance of image from local:  {}.".format(distance_image))
            print()

    process_this_frame = not process_this_frame

    # Display the results
    for (top, right, bottom, left), name in zip(face_locations, face_names):
        # Scale back up face locations since the frame we detected in was scaled to 1/4 size
        top *= 4
        right *= 4
        bottom *= 4
        left *= 4

        # Draw a box around the face
        cv2.rectangle(frame, (left, top), (right, bottom), (0, 0, 255), 2)

        # Draw a label with a name below the face
        cv2.rectangle(frame, (left, bottom - 35), (right, bottom), (0, 0, 255), cv2.FILLED)
        font = cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_DUPLEX
        cv2.putText(frame, name, (left + 6, bottom - 6), font, 1.0, (255, 255, 255), 1)

    # Display the resulting image
    cv2.imshow('Video', frame)

    # Hit 'q' on the keyboard to quit!
    if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
        break

# Release handle to the webcam
video_capture.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

```

Yes, I deliberately moved towards having very few signals. The old signals were confusing, and implemented incorrectly. There wasn't a good, backwards compatible way of fixing them without creating more confusion etc. See https://django-paypal.readthedocs.org/en/stable/release_notes.html#version-0-2

So, before, we had a 'dispatching' mechanism that responded to all the different ways that payments might vary and sent different signals. It was difficult to make this complete and suit everyone's needs. So now we make the user code decide how to dispatch them, which might be slightly more work for a few common cases, but is much less confusing and much more flexible.

So I wouldn't consider adding specific signals for specific types of payment. However, if you need some utility methods on the IPN object that make it easier to distinguish between different types, patches welcome.

I see two possible ways of depicting the universe. If you only have two sets, then just add a third set denoting the universe:

    venn3([set([1,2]), set([2,3]), set([1,2,3,4,5])], ('A', 'B', 'Universe'), ('r', 'g', 'white'))

    vc[0].set_visible(False)

    venn2([set([1,2]), set([2,3])])
    (x1,x2), (y1,y2) = gca().get_xlim(), gca().get_ylim()
    text(x2-0.01, y2-0.01, str(number_of_elements_elsewhere), ha='right', va='top')

@wayneworkman I think you might be misunderstanding the purpose of a refresh token. Refresh tokens are used to obtain new access tokens without requiring the user to enter his credentials. Whereas access tokens are used to access content that is only meant for verified users (protected). So based on that and your use case, both users and devices should have access tokens if they want to access protected content. I would also suggest checking out the links @vimalloc included in his previous comment, adding some sort of custom claim in the access token seems like it could provide the behavior you're looking for. I hope this helps!

Awesome! So it seems everybody agrees to my suggestion. So I wrote a patch, created a branch and turned this issue into a pull request. Feel free to review the changes and/or try out my branch. I want to make sure that I got it right before releasing this, since I would like to avoid another breaking change.

> Seems fine to me to have a breaking change and release a v1.0 after that.

> Not sure if it's worth breaking backwards compatibility but if it's a new major version [...] it seems ok.

I'm not sure if the next release should be 1.0 because of this change. The move from 0.* to 1.0 would indicate the first stable release, which would be a separate topic. On the other hand, I think it is fair to expect some small degree of breaking changes while in early development stages as indicated by a version of 0.*. Not to mention that flake8 itself had larger breaking changes within the same major version in the past.

As for the breaking impact of this change, it doesn't seem to be too severe after all. If you didn't use leading slashes yet, nothing will stop being ignored after this change. Only in corner cases, like when you configured ignores for a script on the top-level, and a script with the same name exists somewhere down the tree, this will cause the same ignores being applied to the latter script as well. The other way around, if you use leading slashes, there is no difference at all before and after this change.

- pylint can detect the following valid cases to skip the check:
 - Use of future unicode literals thanks to @Yajo 
  ```python
from __future__ import unicode_literals  # We don't need `u` prefix in format string to avoid unicode error
'string {}'.format('á')
```
 - `u` prefix in `u'string'.format`
  ```python
u'string {}'.format(u'á')
```

- pylint can't detect the following invalid cases:
 - String variable without `u` prefix
  ```python
string_variable = 'string {}'
string_variable.format(u'á')  # unicode error
```

IMHO the middle point is:
 - Allow `.format`
 - Force use of `from __future__ import unicode_literals` if a `.format` is detected in the code. (everybody wins)

The other problem is that the cache is on the project level, whereas the
block in question might be modified in different ways by different blocks.
We could get around this by including a hash of the code in the cache entry
index, though.

On Tue, Nov 15, 2016 at 8:25 AM, ercoppa <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Invalidating the cache is not a trivial task:
>
>    - a cache entry is indexed by (addr, insn_bytes, max_size, num_inst,
>    thumb, opt_level)
>    - dirty addresses due to a IRSB are tracked by
>    self.state.scratch.dirty_addrs
>    - to invalidate an entry as soon as an address inside it has been
>    touched, we have to search for it in the cache. To do so, we have to
>    generate a cache key. However, we don't know (insn_bytes, max_size,
>    num_inst, thumb, opt_level) used in the past when inserting the
>    entries in the cache. In case of self modifying-code, opt_level=0. But
>    we can't make similar assumptions on the other values since they could
>    change during the lifetime of a project.
>    - another approach could be to invalidate an entry when the lifter
>    looks for it in the cache. Hence, insn_bytes, max_size, num_inst,
>    thumb, opt_level) are known. We then need to check if the one of
>    addresses related to the cache entry has been touched by the program.
>    However, this requires to keep track of dirty address across different
>    IRSB. This seems expensive.

@zhanghang1989 I had this error with latest gluoncv and gluoncv --pre

> First, there seems to be no canonical per-user cache location like rosdep has it, so I would end up storing the cache in a catkin_lint specific location and be unable to take advantage of pre-cached data from other programs.

Perhaps I am misunderstanding it, but isn't that already the case with the files you are downloading individually? AFAICT they all go into a `catkin_lint` specific cache and aren't shared across applications.

> Second, as I understand the code, it merely downloads the package manifests one by one, which I can do myself more efficiently by limiting the download to those packages which I actually need (I only use rosdistro as a last resort if no local information is available, as I feel that the locally installed packages should override the official distribution sources anyway).

This is not actually the case. We explicitly added this to make it more efficient to get many manifest all at once. The build farm compiles a tar.gz of all package manifests once every 5 minutes.

If you only need one or two package manifests it will be on the same order of magnitude as the single cache, but I would encourage you to try the two methods on a package with many dependencies.

`rosinstall_generator`, for instance, downloads this cache every single time (never caches) and it takes less then 5 seconds on my machine (I used `rviz` as an example). However, if I run `catkin_lint` on something like `rviz` where it has to download many package manifests for its dependencies it takes much longer. I ran it on `rviz` after clearing the cache and it took 55 seconds.

> Regarding the cache invalidation, I will implement an --update-rosdistro just to be safe, although I don't expect it will be needed very often: the rosdistro index itself is not cached and will be fetched each time catkin_lint wants to download data, so the cache merely prevents catkin_lint from learning about changes in previously downloaded manifests.

I agree that some cache is probably appropriate, but it's always good to make people aware of the caching and give them an easy option for refreshing it.

Maybe my version is out-of-date but for me it doesn't seem to re-download the index each time.

I believe that using the `rosdistro.get_cached_distribution` method and caching that result would be better than fetching them individually, but I understand that refactoring to do that will take some time and that you've already invested quite a lot in this particular issue.

@kennethreitz @fweidemann14 @kblicharski,
I finally understood. Here is a short rosetta, for someone who is as new to pew and pipenv as me.

| virtualenvwrapper + pip | pew + pipenv |
-----------------------------------|---------------------
| mkvirtualenv |  pew new  |
| cpvirtualenv | pew cp |
| lsvirtualenv | pew ls |
| workon | pew workon |
| deactivate | Ctrl + D / exit |
| pip install | pipenv install |

A few gotchas:
* The `cdproject` equivalent from virtualenvwrapper is missing. But `pew setproject` can do the change to directory once.
* `pew new` does not have a `--system-site-packages` option. Instead there is a separate `pew toggleglobalsitepackages` command.

With this PR, as long as you have `pipenv/vendor/vendor.txt` and `pipenv/patched/patched.txt` containing requirements in them along with `tasks/vendoring/patches/vendor/*.patch` and `tasks/vendoring/patches/patched/*.patch`, the entire vendor and patched directories can be rebuilt from scratch with the versions specified in those requirements files and the patches in the corresponding patch subdirectory. In addition, you can rebuild `safety.zip`.  As long as you have `invoke` installed:

```console
$ inv vendoring.update
$ inv vendoring.update-safety
```

Completely rebuilds the vendor directories and re-downloads licenses.  For packages that don't include licenses in their PyPI uploads, you can specify the download link manually in `tasks/vendoring/__init__.py`.

(After other discussion elsewhere:)

My view of how non-lazy errors should appear to the library user seems different to how the present implementation tries to present them (which is also different from what is really happening).

My view is that non-lazy errors are a request that errors be presented to the user "earlier rather than later" but it shouldn't otherwise affect what parsl is doing, or otherwise affect the enclosing app.

In lazy mode, exceptions are presented to the enclosing python code at fairly well defined points: mostly, when you ask a future for its result. At that point, you might get back an exception saying that some other piece of work has failed - not directly related to that specific future (a dependency exception).

In non-lazy mode, I can imagine the same thing happening:

When a task fails, parsl stops scheduling new tasks, perhaps tries to kill any tasks that are running now...

... but leaves the DFK usable in future, executors running, and reports the error only at the point that the enclosing app asks for a result. (a variant of a dependency exception).

If the app decides that it wants to abort back to a shell prompt, then it can do that (by not catching the exception, by default!) and *that* is when DFK shutdown happens, and  DFK-related cleanups like shutting down managed executors should happen there.

I think this is substantially less "fail hard" than at least what @yadudoc had been aiming for.

@gustavla I think the behavior we're seeing is due to the logic in coremltools, which seems intended but may not be the logic we want going forward:

* If on macOS, attempt to load, validate, and compile the model (so that the `predict` function will be available).
* If on Linux, don't do any of that, and of course there is no `predict` function.

For this, I opened https://github.com/apple/coremltools/issues/198 to track in coremltools; when that's resolved, we can fix this issue by taking a dependency on the coremltools version that has the fix.

I've been looking to finish up the Windows support. I think it makes sense to revisit previous decisions to support Windows activate/deactivate without symlinking (currently `win_conda_bat_redirect` is used instead of symlinks on Windows). Python supports symlinking for Windows 6 (Vista) and newer (https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/os.html#os.symlink). Not sure how much legacy support Conda is expected to provide but choosing to drop anything older than Windows 6 (Vista) support will drastically simplify activate/deactivate on Windows.

This will also open up opportunities to support other important shells (e.g. PowerShell).

Even AppVeyor uses Windows Server 2012 (which corresponds to Windows 8).

This should especially be revistited since according to Anaconda's FAQ only version 2.2 supports Windows XP. So all "modern" Windows since XP should support symlinking.

Yeah, so basically, Postgres will source WAL from the archive (via WAL-E) or from another server (via `primary_conninfo`) and switches somewhat seamlessly between them (whenever one method has an error, the other method is tried, back and forth).

In principle, you only need one or the other. If you use WAL-E only (no `primary_conninfo`) then you will have higher replication latency (only full segments can be applied, and they are polled) and no hot-standby feedback (search for this feature by name if you are curious what it does). But, scalability is effectively unlimited.

By contrast, if one sets up `primary_conninfo` only, one will have streaming replication. The problem is then WAL can only be sourced from what is present on the primary, and recovery can get stuck if the primary no longer has the WAL to replay from the replication position the standby is at.

Some people use the former configuration, I surmise few use the latter arrangement because of limitations in replay. And most have both sources set.

> Hrmm, Travis tests are failing due to being unable to find settings file. Am I doing something incorrect in how I'm getting the cli.Client object?

Yes. And I should've been more precise in my original reply.

As written, `config.get_config()` is executed when the module is imported. This is bad. Code should be executed when the user wants it to, not at import time. This is why this idiom shows up in many applications:

```python
def main():
    do_interesting_stuff

if __name__ == '__main__':
    exit(main())
```

The unittest test runner (and any reasonably compatible clone, such as py.test) execute `setUpModule()` very early during the test execution process. It's probably best to insert code which inspects the runtime environment into such a method. That method can then overwrite or update a global variable. Something like this:

```python
_SELINUX_ENABLED = None

def setUpModule():
    global _SELINUX_ENABLED
    _SELINUX_ENABLED = cli.Client(config.get_config()).run(...)
```

@bolkedebruin the post/get statements in the tests are the mocks used per test case. Each test case contains a single call to the Hook, after which the assertions check that each mock was called once (or in some cases called at all). It can be checked that this is the case by moving the assertions to before the call to the hook, this will cause them to fail.

> It would be preferred to find the library and headers using find_library and find_path, similar to what you actually do in your own Findassimp.cmake: https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/cmake-modules/Findassimp.cmake#L55-L65 

As the `assimp-config.cmake` file is generated by Assimp itself, there is no need to perform a system-wide search using [`find_library`](https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.5/command/find_library.html) and [`find_path`](https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.5/command/find_path.html). The typical solution for this problem is to generate a CMake config file that contains imported targets rather than raw library names, see in CMake documentation https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.11/manual/cmake-packages.7.html and in particular https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.11/manual/cmake-packages.7.html#creating-relocatable-packages .

I removed on my sid the old unmaintained libssl 1.0.0 package, and now I get the error message ```No usable version of the libssl was found```. At least that's not a segfault.
A strace shows that only libssl.so.1.0.0 is searched, with a fallback on libssl.so.10, while the libssl1.0.2 package provides only libssl.so.1.0.2.
I tried to put a symlink, but the symbols changed in libssl 1.0.2 (SSLv3_method missing). That's why Debian did not simply upgrade the previous package and kept my old version installed: OpenSSL broke the binary compatibility…
So right now, .NET Core 2.0 may work on ```Debian 8.(7+)```, not on ```Debian 8.7+``` 
Considering that Debian 9 will be out in the next months, supporting it is imho important. And it will come with OpenSSL 1.0.2 as a fallback so you should be able to use it.
https://packages.debian.org/search?keywords=libssl&searchon=names&suite=testing&section=all

More generally, the `.c` files in `subprocess32` contains lots of checks of the form `#ifdef HAVE_FOO`. However, those cannot be expected to work properly because this backport is missing the `configure` script from Python 3.x which sets all those macros. Some of those macros also appear in Python 2.7, so those are safe. But others like `HAVE_DIRFD` do not exist in Python 2.7.

So I think that you should also use the `configure` script from Python 3.x in `subprocess32`.

Opaque types will never be copied (assuming you take them by pointer or reference); making them opaque is essentially just turning them into ordinary classes that go through the generic type caster.  If they are local, they can't cross the module barrier; if global they can.

So in that respect, the `v4.append` in the tests added in #949 is essentially doing a cross-module global test, and the tests that raise exceptions in `test_local_bindings()` are essentially testing a cross-module failure due to local types that can't cross the barrier.

> Even so, f"-u {os.getuid()}" by default would not seem harmful. 

The problem is that I am not sure the docker image would work with `--user {os.getuid()}`. For example, if the docker image is written to run as `root`, all file system is root, the specified user might not be able to find the program (path problem?), or the program might not be able to write inside the image (cannot create file etc). The same problem exists if the image is designed to run as another user.

So without `-u` we are running the image with their designed user, which does not have problem in running the image but might not be able to write to mounted image.

With `-u` we might not be able to run the image, but should be able to write to mounted image.

@thehackercat I think these points from http://zeromq.org/area:faq fit your case: 

**When sending a multipart message, my receiver doesn't get any message parts until the last part is sent. Is ZeroMQ broken?**

The ZeroMQ library guarantees that multipart messages are sent and received atomically. A message cannot be delivered by the library to your application until all frames have been received. Obviously, this cannot happen while the sender still has parts pending or in queue.

There are two reasons for multipart message support in the library.

This function supports a scatter/gather-style for message transmission so the application can avoid copying the (potentially) large data to a single message. Zero copy is a huge performance win particularly for large messages.
Multipart support may help a protocol designer to logically separate their protocol into separate frames at the application level. This may ease parsing on the receiving end since not all languages support fast bit-twiddling for binary structures like C.

**Does ZeroMQ buffer my entire message in memory before sending to its recipients?**

Yes. So if you are sending multi-gigabyte messages, it will consume RAM to store those messages on both sides. The memory on the sender side is freed after last part of the message has been sent. Also, see point above.

**Is it possible to receive EAGAIN or to block when sending a multi-part message?**

Multipart messages are sent as an atomic unit. If one part is sent successfully, then the socket is guaranteed to not block or return EAGAIN until all parts have been sent. Of course, if you run out of memory in the meantime then the guarantee doesn't mean much, so don't try to send messages that exceed available memory.

**Are you trying to tell me that ZeroMQ won't magically save me from out-of-memory conditions?**

You are a perfect candidate for using AMQP or JMS. Please use Google to find more information on libraries that support those platforms and protocols.

Hi @jeffwidman, and thanks for reaching out. It sounds like you're asking for a way to make pykafka communicate with a newer broker using an older protocol version - for example, talking to a 0.10 broker using the 0.9 protocol. To my knowledge this is not possible. The protocol version used by a broker is defined entirely by the version of kafka that broker is running, and I'm not aware of any mechanism that lets brokers communicate using a different protocol version.

The [versioned changes](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol) to the protocol have so far been backward-compatible with previous versions, which is why our [test suite](https://travis-ci.org/Parsely/pykafka/builds/190323693) succeeds against both kafka 0.8 and 0.10 without needing to tell pykafka itself about which broker version it's communicating with. In fact, with the exception of components using librdkafka, pykafka is as a rule totally unaware of the protocol version of the broker it's talking to.

I hope this helps shed some light on the issue. Please follow up on this ticket if I've missed something - otherwise you can just close it.

Illustration of the necessity to use `getattr`:

```python
import torch

class MyModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(MyModule, self).__init__()
        self.layers = []
        for k in range(1):
            self.layers.append(torch.nn.Linear(5, 5))
            self.add_module('layer_{}'.format(k), self.layers[-1])

        self.parameter = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.rand(5))

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.layers[0](x)


model = MyModule()
model.cuda(0)

# replicate to GPUs 0 and 1
replicas = torch.nn.parallel.replicate(model, [0, 1])

# replicas[0] is the model on GPU0
# replicas[1] is the model on GPU1
print("Model replica and location of parameter attribute")
for k in range(2):
    print(k, replicas[k].parameter.get_device())

# except!
print("Location of replicas[1].layers[0].weight ", replicas[1].layers[0].weight.get_device())
print("Location of replicas[1].layers[0].weight using getattr", getattr(replicas[1], 'layer_0').weight.get_device())
```

There was a change going from 2.4.5 to 3.0.0 where `extra_vars` are disallowed unless the job template author either specifies "Prompt on Launch" or creates and enables a survey. If a survey is enabled and the "Prompt on Launch" is false, then only the survey variables will be accepted, while non-survey variables will be ignored.

The intent of this change was to avoid giving permissions to job template users that are not obvious to the people who set it up. This information should be in the release notes somewhere, but I can see how it's hard to confirm exactly with the [API guide](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible-tower/2.2.0/html/userguide/job_templates.html). We might do better to describe the specific case for extra_vars, beyond the general rules for prompted and un-prompted variables.

> I'm not sure what the story is here, i.e. how it got out of sync. I think the best fix might be to pass in inputs, outputs, because otherwise the Python regions have no way to access inputs/outputs during initialization (whereas C++ encoders can call getInput and getOutput). It's important to be able to access inputs during initialization because, for example, the SPRegion needs to tell the Spatial Pooler the input width.

Now that networks have a first-class `getLinks()` API, we can probably accomplish what we want without having to rely on the arguments passed to `network.initialize()`.

To that end, I propose to resolve this specific issue:

0. Reimplement the `PyRegion.initialize()` abstract method as:

    ```python
      @abstractmethod
      def initialize(self):
        """Initialize the node after the network is fully linked
        It is called once by NuPIC before the first call to compute(). It is
        a good place to perform one time initialization that depend on the inputs
        and/or outputs. The region may also remember its inputs and outputs here
        because they will not change.
        """
    ```
0. Remove the logic in PyRegion.cpp that passes `dims` and `splitterMaps`
0. Separately, address the issues of relationships between inputs and outputs in the initialization methods of specific regions where it matters

The language of (re.complement (str.to.re "A")) is the set of strings:
     Sigma* \ { "A" } = { "", "B", "C", "AA", "AB", .... }
where Sigma is the set of characters (8 -bit bit-vectors).
This language includes the empty string.
Similarly the second example, you can take a non-zero number of repetitions of the empty string and you still get the empty string. so the empty string is also in (re.+ (re.complement (str. to.re "A")))

  accuracy_const, accuracy_update_const = tf.metrics.accuracy(

  with tf.control_dependencies([accuracy_update_const]):

  accuracy_ph, accuracy_update_ph = tf.metrics.accuracy(
      labels_ph, logits_ph, name="acc_ph")
  with tf.control_dependencies([accuracy_update_ph]):

The important semantic part of `loop` (as a verb) is that `BaseService` would keep running the method over and over until the service shut down. I haven't found another word that conveys that as well. `daemon` itself kind of implies that, but usually a `daemon` would handle its own looping, which would be a lot less convenient. Maybe:

- `cycle_task`
- `repeat_task`
- `repeat_forever`

Simplifying them to something like the following might make a lot of sense:

- if it fails decoding: no assertions (unless we can think of good ones)
- assertions that numeric values are strictly positive for *unsigned* types.
- *maybe* assertions that decoded values are negative if we can reliably look at the right bit.
- assertions that numeric values are in the appropriate range for the given type.
- assertions that the value is an integer for types which should only yield integers.
- assertions that the resulting *type* is of the appropriate type for the decoder.
- assertions that on encoding the resulting value is the right length for any fixed length encoders.

We found the root cause of this issue, but it was not directly related to pyserial.
In our case, the serial port was connected over LPC(low pin count) bus. On the same bus/chip we had the fan controller.
Due to some incorrect behavior of the fan controller, it flooded the bus and the interrupts that were triggered by the serial port were never send back to the CPU.
Even if the issue was not pyserial, IMHO, the lib should have timeout function/behavior. 

It might be the same issue on your side, interrupts that are not received by Linux side.

I'm using 1.34, so that may be part of it.  I have a master flac tree in [artist]/[album]/*.flac.  I had an old script that created a similar mp3 tree based on that flac tree.  That script used id3v2 to embed front cover art with no desc.

I'm updating that script to add musicbrainz, ReplayGain, and additional tags, and with more robust coverart change detection for when I update the master cover art image, with the goal of deprecating id3v2.  I was testing APIC:None, and if it wasn't there I was writing the cover image to APIC:None, but with a spec with a desc.  That left me with two identical, but individually valid, front cover tags.

I hacked around this by using id3v2 to detect multiple front cover images so my script could delete all APIC and then just properly embed the correct one via `current.m['APIC:Front cover'] = mutagen.id3.APIC(enc, 'image/jpeg', 3, 'Front cover', current.imagedata)`

Hi @ellisonbg, I am doing great, I hope you are well too. Thanks for the interest. To answer your questions:

* We would need to see. The symengine's Basic class is here: https://github.com/symengine/symengine.py/blob/3fd1e481cf2d3f6b37ec72f8ba89b75ced587283/symengine/lib/symengine_wrapper.pyx#L760, I think you can subclass it in Python. But I don't know what exact things you need for quantum.

* That depends if the speed of sympy.quantum is sufficient. If not, where is the bottleneck? If SymPy operations, then it makes sense to port on top of symengine.

* As you mentioned, https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/13189 is a start. Then we'll go from there.

* We haven't implemented assumptions yet, I just made an issue for it: https://github.com/symengine/symengine/issues/1334

In general, I would add, that even though you were initially reserved about using C++ for symengine, it worked really well. The main library (https://github.com/symengine/symengine) is just C++, no Python, and it's pretty clean, and as you can see, lots of people have contributed to it, and students in general have no problems getting up to speed. The modern subset of C++ that we use allows us to be pretty much safe in Debug mode, just like in Python, for all practical purposes. In Release mode there doesn't seem to be any sacrifices in speed that we had to make. It is also completely multiplatform, it works with all major compilers. The main thing however is the speed (in Release mode). I want to implement more systematic benchmarks (https://github.com/symengine/symengine/issues/1318), but from what we ran by hand, it looks like we are one of the fastest, if not the fastest.

So if you need speed, symengine is the way to go. For quantum, do you have some test cases which you want to make faster? One can implement that in C++, then one can concentrate on the speed, data structures, algorithms, and nail it. It's work, but if speed is what you want, I think that's the best way to go. If you only need fast symbolics, then use symengine from Python, and build the rest on top in Python. That's much easier, and delivers most of the time.
I will take a look and get back to you as soon as I can, thanks for the report!

@kmunson1973 

You can only do this with a transaction. The reason is as follows.

As you saw when you tried to delete TCP, BIG-IP re-created it. Then when you tried to add wom-tcp... it rejected it saying it already exists.

Putting it in a transaction lets BIG-IP figure it out correctly, see below example.

```
from f5.bigip import BigIP
from f5.bigip.contexts import TransactionContextManager

# Connect to the BigIP
bip = BigIP("localhost", "admin", "admin", port='10443')

# Create a transaction
tx = bip.transactions.transaction

# Perform the next set of operations from within the above transaction
with TransactionContextManager(tx) as api:

    # select the virtual I want to modify
    virt = api.ltm.virtuals.virtual.load(name='virtual-1')

    # Get a reference to its profiles
    profiles = virt.profiles_s.profiles

    # Load the TCP profile so I can delete it
    tcp = profiles.load(name='tcp')

    # Delete said profile
    tcp.delete()

    # The delete operation changes the list of profiles internally
    # so re-get the list of profiles. Note, this may be a bug with our
    # SDK...so I'll investigate
    profiles = virt.profiles_s.profiles

    # Create the new profile
    profiles.create(name='wom-tcp-wan-optimized')
```

When the Transaction context finishes, the transaction will commit and you'll get the desired behavior. Note that it's kinda similar to what you're doing with PATCH, it's just that with the Transaction method you don't need to keep track of what currently is there. Just remove what you dont need and create what you do.

Hope that helps.

As I get working on a PR, I have got some second thoughts, hope to discuss more with you guys @luca-s @twiecki @ssanderson .

The current proposal we've agreed on:
```Python
# implemented as current get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns and signature except
# for forward_returns computation and periods
get_clean_factor(factor, forward_returns, ....):
    ...

# same function signature as current implementation but it would call get_clean_factor
get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns(factor, prices, periods, ....):
    forward_returns = ... # compute forward_returns
    return get_clean_factor(factor, forward_returns, ....)
```

There are 3 kinds of logic here:
- take prices, compute forward returns, return a `forward_returns` data frame
- take a `factor` series, return a data frame containing the original factor, the quantized version, and optionally the grouping infomation
- combine the `forward_returns` and the factor data frame into a single `factor_data` data frame, report  data loss information

Is it a better idea to make `get_clean_factor` only responsible for second logic, i.e. quantizing factor, and have a new function `get_clean_factor_data` (ignore the naming for the moment) deal with the third one? Slightly different from the current proposal, but seems much cleaner. Something like this:
```Python
# get_clean_factor is only responsible for processing factor
get_clean_factor(factor, quantiles, groupby, group_labels, ....):
    ...

# get_clean_factor_data can be used to combine different sets of
# pre-computed factor and forward_returns
# arguments related to processing factor and forward_returns are
# completely factored out, only max_loss remain
get_clean_factor_data(factor, forward_returns, max_loss):
    factor = get_clean_factor(factor, ....)
    merged_data = ... # merge forward_returns and factor

    # handling data loss information
    ...

# get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns keeps the current signature
# but implemented as a simple wrapper
get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns(factor, prices, periods, ....):
    forward_returns = ... # compute forward_returns
    factor = get_clean_factor(factor, forward_returns, ...)
    factor_data = get_clean_factor_data(factor, forward_returns)
    return factor_data
    ...
```

If this is the right way to go, there's something I am not sure. Currently, computing quantized factor involves calling `utils.quantize_factor`, whose `factor_data` argument assumes that the factor series is already merged into a data frame with forward_returns and grouping columns. It does almost the same thing as `get_clean_factor(factor, quantiles, groupby, group_labels, ....)`.

Your code works in Python 2, but breaks in Python 3 because it auto-sets `__hash__ = None` when `__eq__` is defined for a class (as is the case in Schematics `ErrorMessage`) - you need to provide your own hash method.

In Python an object can only be [hashable](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-hashable) if it has a hash value which never changes during its lifetime, that is, it is immutable.

We have a "problem" right now with the Errors in that they are mutable (they contain a list of messages and a `pop` method that changes it).

Also, hashable objects which compare equal must have the same hash value, and we use the instance `__dict__` for comparison inside `__eq__`, which is also mutable/unhashable.

Python itself does not require Exceptions to be immutable. At least in 2002, GVR was in favor of mutable exceptions: https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-dev/2002-August/027900.html

The `logging` framework assumes exceptions are hashable (and thus immutable), which is an undocumented assumption. But since logging is in the stdlib, we need to play by their rules.

The fix to support logging is to make all schematics exceptions immutable. I don't have a proposal for how to do this, but would welcome a PR.

Hey all! I've taken your feedback in to account and tweaked the pull request. As it stands, I've made only very minor tweaks to the code itself. The majority of my changes have to do with documentation. I've added a short section in to the "examples" document and fiddled with many of the docstrings. There are three changes of particular note:

First, I've made changes to the "Examples" document.

Second, I've expanded the docstring for method `EntitySearchMixin.search_payload` and added several links pointing to it. It now gives more insight in to what the final search queries look li.e

Third, I'm now referencing `LifecycleEnvironment` in my examples instead of `Organization`. Why? Because this is an invalid search:

    GET /katello/api/v2/organizations
    {'name': 'foo', 'description': 'bar'}  # server ignores these params

But this is a valid search:

    GET /katello/api/v2/lifecycle_environments
    {'name': 'foo', 'organization_id': 1}  # server accepts these params

The default implementation of `search_payload` is pretty naive. But I at least want users to be able to copy and paste the examples in the documentation!

Fascinating!

I decided to calculated center of mass slightly differently. Instead of taking the average of the neighbors' positions, I used the SciPy `ndimage.measurements.center_of_mass` function. Now, I might have done this wrong, but it's now producing the opposite behavior as before; all boids head to the upper-right. My `cohere` code is below, the rest is the same as the update to boid.py.

```Python
    def cohere(self, neighbors):
        """
        Return the vector toward the center of mass of the local neighbors.
        """
        from scipy import ndimage
        vec = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
        their_pos = [neighbor.pos for neighbor in neighbors]
        their_pos = np.asarray(their_pos)
        center = ndimage.measurements.center_of_mass(their_pos)
        vec += np.subtract(center, self.pos)  # both are tuples
        if np.linalg.norm(vec) > 0:
            return vec / np.linalg.norm(vec)
        else:
            return vec
```

Since then I played with the concept a bit more and ended up [applying it to asyncio more seriously](https://github.com/Tygs/ayo).

I'm definitely biased to the name "Execution scope" now. Indeed, the scope already has the notion of nesting, and boundaries and is well know in programming. Putting "execution" in front of it make it clear it's a specific kind of scope that limit the execution of something.

This translate into quite a clear API IMO. [Example](https://github.com/Tygs/ayo#execution-scope-aka-trios-nursery) from the asyncio lib experiment: 

```python
import random
import asyncio
import ayo

async def zzz():
    time = random.randint(0, 15)
    await asyncio.sleep(time)
    print(f'Slept for {time} seconds')

async with ayo.scope(max_concurrency=10, timeout=12) as run:
    for _ in range(20):
        run.asap(zzz()) # schedule the coroutine for execution
```

Not only the implementation works, but it also runs as it reads, which is a great benefit for a new concept.

This allows to build even higher level API on top of it:

```python
async with ayo.scope() as run:
    run.all(zzz(), zzz(), zzz())
    run << zzz() # shorcut for run.asap(zzz())
    run.aside(callback, foo, bar=1)  # run.asap(asyncio.run_in_executor(None, callback, foo, bar=1))
    run.after(2, callback, foo, bar=1)
    run.at(dt.datetime(2018, 12, 1), callback, foo, bar=1)
    run.every(0.2, callback, foo, bar=1)
```

"Run in this execution scope" is, IMO, quite a clear way to express all this.

@floringogianu I went ahead to test the same setup and script on a large-memory instance, which proceeds to complete fine without the above errors.  The tldr is, instead of `policy = ray.experimental.SimpleGcsFlushPolicy()`, do `policy = ray.experimental.SimpleGcsFlushPolicy(flush_period_secs=0.1)`.  The difference is the former uses a default value of flushing redis for some amount of data every 10 seconds, which is too slow for your application.  Details in [this section](http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/redis-memory-management.html#paramaters-of-the-flushing-policy).

With this change, @ 4M steps I see ~4.8GB of memory usage from redis-server.  With the original flush period of 10s, @ 4M steps its memory usage is ~7.8GB.

Here's how I arrived at this parameter value.  I eyeballed that on my system, the script executes about 5K steps/sec, corresponding to 50K Ray tasks/sec (the `remote()` calls).  A reasonable rule of thumb is to multiply this with 2 or 3 to get the _desired flush rate_.  Let's say I want ~100K entries/sec to be my flush rate, then setting `flush_period_secs=0.1` does the trick -- I won't bother you with more details since it's in that page, but you can have other parameters to play with too!

This is my strawman attempt at solving this.  You might want to do a combination of `flush_period_secs` and `flush_num_entries_each_time` to achieve an even lower memory threshold for redis. Please let us know if that works.

On each node in the cluster, there is a single **object store** (we use the name **Plasma** to refer to the object store).

Whenever an object store becomes full, it simply evicts a bunch of objects (to free up about 20% of its memory).

If you later call `ray.get` on an object ID that corresponds to an object that has been evicted, then the task that created the object will be rerun to recreate the object.

There are currently a couple limitations here.

1. If the task that created the evicted object is nondeterministic, then when the task is rerun, it may produce a different result (a warning will be logged in this case).
2. If the object was not created by a task but rather was passed as an argument into a remote function or created by a call to `ray.put` on the driver process, it may not be recreated.

The advantage here is the simplicity (we've also experimented with tracking global reference counts). There are some limitations as well of course since we may have to reconstruct objects. Are you running into situations where objects you need later are being evicted?

Quick answer : update to capstone 4.0.alpha2

Long answer :
Problem comes with instruction `and	rax, 0xfc000000` or "48 25 00 00 00 FC"
Immediate operand does not get sign extended
So rax which was before 0x00007ffff80008b0 becomes 0x00000000f8000000 instead of  0x00007ffff8000000
Therefore, `mov	rcx, qword ptr [rax]` accesses the wrong memory, which happens to be invalid in this case.
Doing something similar as operand sign-extension in ADC instruction, I found out that capstone was telling that immediate operand was 8-byte sized.
I guess that the commit which fixed it was 
https://github.com/aquynh/capstone/commit/de8dd26780efc2fd32fe4329ddc8f11769b4d238
You can test capstone with `cstool -d x64 "48 25 00 00 00 FC"` and see if you get `and	rax, 0xfffffffffc000000`or `and	rax, 0xfc000000`

> I have a question though, I am not sure I can see where _MemoryFile objects actually close their _bytes_io buffer. This does not really matter I suppose, since the buffer will be flushed nevertheless when the _MemoryFile is garbage collected, and the BytesIO garbage collected in turn.

IIRC the _MemoryFile references a BytesIO that is stored in the MemoryFS. So it isn't _MemoryFile's responsibility to close it.

But it doesn't look like MemoryFS ever explicitly closes anything. So you're right, it will only be freed when it is garbage collected. It would probably be better to close all the BytesIO objects when MemoryFS.close is called.

  - https://github.com/uqfoundation/klepto/blob/master/klepto/_archives.py
  - https://github.com/uqfoundation/klepto/blob/master/klepto/_cache.py

What `__reduce__` does is help you declare what's needed to identify what the state of the object is, and when put in the form that `__reduce__` requires, it should serialize and save all the necessary state the object needs.

@syrusakbary That doesn't work unfortunately either. I have thought about that a bit more and would you be open to introducing some kind of metadata concept that we can store on the field definition? It would not be to difficult to implement and you would simply have a `set_metadata` method that would set a `_metadata` attribute and then we can just make sure to carry around this `_metadata` attribute. This would then actually make it a feature other people could use as well.

To give an example I could have:

```py
def set_metadata(gql_type, value):
    gql_type.set_metadata(value)
    return gql_type

class MyObjectType(graphene.ObjectType):
    my_string = set_metadata(graphene.String(), 'some_value')
    mg_string = set_metadata(graphene.Field(myScalar), 'some_other_value')
```

and internally we would have:
```py
class UnmountedType(OrderedType):
    '''
    This class acts a proxy for a Graphene Type, so it can be mounted
    dynamically as Field, InputField or Argument.

    Instead of writing
    >>> class MyObjectType(ObjectType):
    >>>     my_field = Field(String(), description='Description here')

    It let you write
    >>> class MyObjectType(ObjectType):
    >>>     my_field = String(description='Description here')
    '''

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(UnmountedType, self).__init__()
        self.args = args
        self.kwargs = kwargs
        self._metadata = None

    def get_type(self):
        raise NotImplementedError("get_type not implemented in {}".format(self))

    def set_metadata(value)
        self._metadata = value

    def Field(self):  # noqa: N802
        '''
        Mount the UnmountedType as Field
        '''
        from .field import Field
        f = Field(
            self.get_type(),
            *self.args,
            _creation_counter=self.creation_counter,
            **self.kwargs
        )
        f.set_metadata(self._metadata)
        return f

    def InputField(self):  # noqa: N802
        '''
        Mount the UnmountedType as InputField
        '''
        from .inputfield import InputField
        f = InputField(
            self.get_type(),
            *self.args,
            _creation_counter=self.creation_counter,
            **self.kwargs
        )
        f.set_metadata(self._metadata)
        return f

    def Argument(self):  # noqa: N802
        '''
        Mount the UnmountedType as Argument
        '''
        from .argument import Argument
        a = Argument(
            self.get_type(),
            *self.args,
            _creation_counter=self.creation_counter,
            **self.kwargs
        )
        a.set_metadata(self_metadata)
        return a
```

And `Argument`, `Field` and `InputField` would of course implement these methods accordingly. We could even allow it being set at instantiation to keep it a one-liner. What do you think? Imo a worthy feature to add and will keep everything else exactly as it currently is.

@hackaugusto It should not be updated so often. The `eth_gasPrice()` won't return a much changed gas price so often. Checking once every 10 minutes is fine, and could even be too often. The gas price is not a property of the block. It's something that the client calculates itself and depends on the last N blocks. I think N is 100.

Here is how geth [is supposed to calculate it](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/wiki/Gas-Price-Oracle). Parity (I think) has a gas percentile of the last 100 blocks as a command line option. And it is [set by default to 50](https://github.com/paritytech/parity/pull/7497/files#diff-54d6bbd5addb7a0d39c647e4e33e6990R700) right now.

1) hash the `channel_address` into the signature. That is sign sha3(msg+channel_address).

Ok glad to see this thread is alive!

@bocklund can you submit a separate issue about chaining of FWs? I think we all want to the FWs to be chain-able (i.e., have the inputs of one FW be consistent with the output of another). But, since we can identify this as a clear and separate issue, we can create one for it.

I want to clarify the purpose of atomate. Yes, we want to standardize. But we also want to make going off the beaten path simple and intuitive - and to make this a natural and expected thing for people to do. The design of atomate would be simple if it was just about calculating pre-defined recipes.

For example, here's the type of thing that I would expect atomate to do well:

- Someone computes a GGA band structure without even thinking about it
- Next, we realize that vdW corrections might be important. It should be easy to run the same workflow but with a vdW functional - ideally without knowing too much about exactly which INCAR parameters are associated with each vdW functional and just by listing your vdW functional as a String. Meaning, I want to use a ``vdw="optB88" `` type of syntax like ``MPRelaxSet`` has in pymatgen, not a ``user_incar_settings={"LUSE_VDW": True, "AGGAC": 0.0, "GGA": "BO", "PARAM1": 0.18333333, "PARAM2": 0.22}`` syntax. Obviously one needs to draw a line as to at what point things should be easy like a string versus custom; to me, this vdW example still falls under a 'don't make me think' operation.
- Similarly, if we want to get an HSE approximation to the gap, it should be clear (i) what the different approximations are (e.g., gap correction only vs full HSE) and (ii) exactly how to run it without needing to pore over details of the code or again needing to use ``user_incar_settings``. Again, I consider this under a 'don't make me think or know all the VASP parameters' example.
- Where things become very custom IMO is if someone wants to mess with the HSE mixing parameter for HF. At that point, I think it's reasonable for someone to have to learn the VASP tag for it and override the ``user_incar_settings`` themselves.

Maybe what we need is to ditch the input sets like @shyuep says and just have atomate make a collection of preset incar dict overrides that represent switching functional or doing common "don't make me think" operations. These dict overrides maybe will read pymatgen data (e.g., so we don't recode all the various vdW INCAR parameters) but don't depend on the user creating an input set that represents what they want. Since people will also want to override kpoints and potcars we should probably prefer ``vasp_input_set_params`` instead of ``user_incar_settings``.

Thus, given all this, the sketch of the code would be:

- each FW follows a consistent signature (to the extent possible), is chainable (to the extent possible)
- each FW hard-codes the input set that is used for that operation. If the FW wants to, it can have some parameter like "functional" that @shyuep proposes to switch between multiple input sets. Up for discussion still is whether such a parameter could also allow a custom input set rather than just toggle between a few presets. However, there is something to be said for having just a single correct way to do things rather than the option to either (i) always use the default vaspinputset and override to customize vs (ii) override the vaspinput set, plus possible customizations on top of that. Will need to think and discuss this.
- if you want to do something other than the hard-coded input set, feel free to override with ``vasp_input_set_params``.
- Atomate does something to make exploring a little off the beaten path (e.g., switching to a vdW functional) less painless rather than having to always explicitly set VASP INCAR parameters. e.g., collections of common ``vasp_input_set_params`` for doing things like stricter convergence or switching the functional that aren't as painful as manually setting each VASP tag individually.

I was able to reproduce your problem, it also happens outside of Jupyter. You're right about the difference in the values in `dt_index`, and that's also the problem.

`pandas.date_range()` returns a `DatetimeIndex` object. That calls has a `strftime` method that expects a standard Python datetime formatter, which as far as I know doesn't have a unix timestamp directive. So if using `'%s'` worked before, it seems undocumented behavior.

A quick solution I found is to convert the `DatetimeIndex` object to integer values (unix time in nanoseconds) and then to strings:
```
datetime_index = pd.date_range('2016-1-1', periods=n_periods, freq='M')
dt_index_ints = datetime_index.astype(int) // 10**9
dt_index = dt_index_ints.astype(str)
```

Can you check if that works for you? Are there other solutions for this?
